text.title: Heuristic Evaluation — Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Heuristic Evaluation — Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The evaluation MUST be run by 3-5 expert evaluators independently. Fewer than 3 is rejected.
rule.rationale: Single-evaluator audits are biased; 3-5 evaluators catch ~75% of problems while ≥6 yields diminishing returns.

rule.statement: Every screen / surface in scope MUST be audited against ALL 10 heuristics. Partial-heuristic audits are rejected.
rule.rationale: Single-heuristic focus misses orthogonal failure modes; the heuristic set was designed to be applied as a whole.

rule.statement: Evaluators MUST audit independently first, then reconcile findings in a joint session. Single-pass joint audits are rejected.
rule.rationale: Independent audits surface different findings; joint-first sessions converge on the first speaker's opinions.

rule.statement: Every finding MUST carry a severity (cosmetic / minor / major / catastrophic) and a heuristic id. Unrated or unmapped findings are rejected.
rule.rationale: Severity drives prioritisation; heuristic mapping makes the finding categorisable and trackable.

rule.statement: Every finding MUST carry a concrete recommendation. Diagnostic-only "this is bad" findings are rejected.
rule.rationale: Recommendations turn the report into a backlog; diagnostics alone do not.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
